title: OXMIQ Raises $35 Million to Scale OxCore™ Architecture
gist: OXMIQ Labs, Raja Koduri's licensable GPU/AI architecture startup, closed a $35M Series A ($60M total raised) co-led by Fundomo and Samsung Catalyst Fund, with MediaTek, Intel Capital, Pegatron and others betting on an open, configurable, CUDA-compatible compute IP model that lets any design team build custom AI silicon without a full chip program.

insights.claim: OXMIQ Labs closed a $35 million Series A financing, bringing total capital raised to $60 million, co-led by Fundomo and Samsung Catalyst Fund.
insights.speaker: Daniel Nenni
insights.so_what: A relatively modest raise signals an IP-first, capital-efficient model rather than a full-fab capex play; track dilution and burn against milestone delivery.
insights.quote: today closed its $35 million Series A financing, bringing the company's total capital raised to $60 million

insights.claim: OXMIQ's round drew a strategically loaded cap table spanning Samsung Catalyst Fund, MediaTek (reinvesting seed investor), Pegatron Venture Capital, CDIB-TEN, Darwin Ventures, Morgan Creek Digital, AM Intelligence Labs, and Intel Capital as a strategic IP partner.
insights.speaker: Daniel Nenni
insights.so_what: The investor mix concentrates Taiwan/Korea supply-chain and ODM players plus Intel and Samsung, suggesting OXMIQ is being positioned as an anti-Nvidia open-architecture bet across the incumbent supply chain.
insights.locator: The round was co-led by Fundomo and Samsung Catalyst Fund

insights.claim: MediaTek, a seed investor and leading fabless firm, is reinvesting in OXMIQ to extend its on-device AI ambitions from edge to cloud.
insights.speaker: Daniel Nenni
insights.so_what: MediaTek doubling down flags this architecture as a candidate for edge/on-device inference where MediaTek competes; watch for OXMIQ IP appearing in MediaTek roadmaps.
insights.quote: We see this investment as a way to continue unlocking unprecedented on-device AI performance across all technology platforms.

insights.claim: OXMIQ's OxCore is a licensable GPU core integrating three engines on one core—a CUDA-compatible GPU engine, a tensor processing engine, and an orchestration CPU—tightly coupling functionality typically split across three chips and purpose-built for near-memory compute to minimize data movement.
insights.speaker: Daniel Nenni
insights.so_what: Near-memory, three-engines-on-one-core is a differentiated energy-efficiency pitch for agentic inference; the key risk to monitor is whether it beats Nvidia on real workloads, not FPGA demos.
insights.quote: OxCore tightly couples compute functionality that is typically split across three chips, and was purpose-built for near-memory compute, minimizing data movement

insights.claim: OxCore is currently running only on FPGA with live demonstrations, not yet in silicon.
insights.speaker: Daniel Nenni
insights.so_what: FPGA-only maturity means real performance, PPA, and tape-out risk are unproven; discount claims until first customer silicon.
insights.quote: OxCore is running on FPGA today, with live demonstrations available.

insights.claim: OXMIQ's OxQuilt chiplet architecture is designed to adapt to any supply chain—any foundry, memory type, interconnect standard, or advanced packaging option—rather than locking a design to a specific foundry and memory type as most AI silicon does.
insights.speaker: Daniel Nenni
insights.so_what: Supply-chain-agnostic packaging is a hedge against foundry/HBM concentration and export-control fragmentation; appeals to teams wanting to avoid single-vendor lock-in.
insights.quote: Most AI silicon designs are locked to a specific foundry and memory type. OxQuilt instead adapts to any supply chain

insights.claim: OXMIQ's OxPython software runs existing CUDA and PyTorch code on OxCore without code changes, aiming for full developer portability and day-zero support for new models.
insights.speaker: Daniel Nenni
insights.so_what: Frictionless CUDA/PyTorch portability directly attacks Nvidia's software moat—the single biggest switching cost in AI compute; if real, it lowers the barrier to alternative silicon adoption.
insights.quote: OxPython™ runs existing CUDA® and PyTorch® code on OxCore without code changes
insights.locator: OXMIQ pairs the hardware with a software stack

insights.claim: OXMIQ pursues an IP-first, capital-efficient model, generating revenue from customer engagements and licensing rather than building full SoCs, preserving capital for stack development.
insights.speaker: Daniel Nenni
insights.so_what: An Arm-style licensing model in AI compute could scale with less capex risk; monitor whether licensing revenue actually materializes given only $60M raised.
insights.quote: By focusing on new architecture IP rather than full SoC development, the company generates revenue from customer engagements while preserving capital for building the stack.
insights.locator: OXMIQ's IP-first model is built for capital efficiency.

insights.claim: OXMIQ frames the core market problem as token demand outpacing the world's ability to build infrastructure to serve it, positioning cost-of-intelligence reduction at every layer as the opportunity.
insights.speaker: Daniel Nenni
insights.so_what: The 'drive down cost of intelligence' framing is the investable thesis behind alt-silicon; a bet that inference economics, not raw training FLOPs, define the next decade.
insights.quote: Token demand is outpacing the world's ability to build infrastructure to serve it.

insights.claim: Fundomo partner Rajeev Surati argues most compute IP forces customers to bend memory, packaging, and foundry around the chip, whereas OXMIQ inverts this and turns a cost center into leverage.
insights.speaker: Daniel Nenni
insights.so_what: The investment thesis rests on flexibility-as-leverage; a differentiated framing worth testing against actual design-win economics.
insights.quote: Most compute IP makes the customer bend their memory, packaging, and foundry around the chip. OXMIQ does the opposite, and that flips a cost center into leverage.
insights.locator: Raja has built silicon at every layer

insights.claim: OXMIQ added Jim Keller, CEO of Tenstorrent, to its board and Intel process-technology Fellow Dr. Valluri (Bob) Rao as an advisor, deepening its chip-architecture pedigree alongside founder Raja Koduri.
insights.speaker: Daniel Nenni
insights.so_what: Marquee architect signaling (Keller + Koduri) is a talent-credibility signal that de-risks execution perception and aids customer/investor recruitment.

insights.claim: OXMIQ positions itself explicitly against industry concentration, framing an open, configurable GPU architecture as a counter to a handful of incumbents controlling AI compute.
insights.speaker: Daniel Nenni
insights.so_what: The 'open vs. incumbents' narrative directly targets Nvidia's dominance; a rallying thesis for strategic investors seeking supply-chain optionality.
insights.quote: As the industry concentrates around a few incumbents, this is more important than ever.
insights.locator: I am excited to join the OXMIQ board

insights.claim: OXMIQ's stack spans the full AI stack from renewable power and data-center infrastructure to silicon IP and 'electron-to-token machines,' with founder Koduri tying cost reduction to democratizing who can build with AI.
insights.speaker: Daniel Nenni
insights.so_what: The vertical 'atoms to agents' scope is ambitious for a $60M company; watch for focus risk, though the AM Green tie-in to a 5GW India AI factory hints at real energy-linked demand.
insights.locator: OXMIQ's expertise spans the full AI stack

insights.claim: AM Intelligence Labs' participation extends a collaboration on a 5GW AI factory initiative in India, including a 3GW renewable-powered AI compute platform being built by AM Green Group.
insights.speaker: Daniel Nenni
insights.so_what: A concrete gigawatt-scale deployment anchor in India links OXMIQ compute IP to renewable energy buildout; a potential early demand pipeline to track.
insights.quote: the 5GW AI factory initiative, including a 3GW renewable-powered AI compute platform, that AMI is building in India
insights.locator: AM Intelligence Labs, part of the AM Green Group
